ideas.Hylomorphism (matter and form):
Hylomorphism — from the Greek *hylē* (matter) and *morphē* (form) — is Aristotle's foundational analysis of what a concrete thing *is*. Against Plato, who located true reality in transcendent Forms separate from sensible things, Aristotle argues that form and matter are inseparable aspects of the individual substances we actually encounter. Every physical object is a compound of **matter** (what it is made of) and **form** (the structure, organization, or essence that makes it the kind of thing it is).

A bronze statue is bronze (matter) shaped into a figure (form); a living organism is flesh and bone (matter) organized by its soul (form) — for Aristotle the **soul** just *is* the form of a living body, its principle of life and function. Neither element exists on its own in nature: matter without form is mere unrealized potential, and form without matter is an abstraction. The form is not in a Platonic heaven but *in* the thing, as its actuality.

This doctrine is the hinge of Aristotle's whole philosophy. It lets him keep Plato's insight that intelligible structure is real — knowledge is knowledge of form — while grounding it in the concrete individual rather than a separate world. It explains change as matter taking on new form, underwrites his biology, psychology, and metaphysics, and gives the medievals their model of the human being as a single form-matter unity of soul and body. Hylomorphism dominated Western thought until the seventeenth century, when Descartes and the mechanists banished "substantial forms" in favor of matter in motion.
ideas.The four causes:
Aristotle's doctrine of the four causes is his answer to what it means to *explain* something. To have full knowledge of a thing, he holds, is to grasp its "why" — and there are four irreducibly different kinds of "why," four **causes** (*aitiai*, better rendered "explanatory factors"). The **material cause** is what a thing is made of (the bronze of a statue). The **formal cause** is its form or essence (the shape and definition that make it *this* thing). The **efficient cause** is the agent or process that brought it about (the sculptor at work). And the **final cause** (*telos*) is the end or purpose for which it exists (to honor a god, to be beautiful).

A complete explanation cites all four. For a natural organism, the material cause is its tissues, the formal cause its species-form or soul, the efficient cause its parents, and the final cause the mature functioning toward which it develops. Aristotle insists the **final cause** is often the most important: nature does nothing in vain, and to understand a living thing is above all to understand what it is *for*.

This teleological framework — the conviction that purpose is woven into nature itself — became the great target of the Scientific Revolution, which banished final causes from physics and kept only material and efficient ones. But Aristotle's fourfold scheme remains a permanent analysis of the different senses of "cause," and his emphasis on function still shapes biology and the philosophy of mind, where the question "what is it *for*?" refuses to disappear.
ideas.Act and potency:
Aristotle's distinction between **potentiality** (*dynamis*) and **actuality** (*energeia* / *entelecheia*) is the conceptual tool with which he solves the ancient problem of change that had paralyzed Parmenides. Parmenides argued that change is impossible, because a thing cannot come from what-is-not. Aristotle answers that between sheer being and sheer non-being lies a third condition: **potential** being. An acorn is not *actually* an oak, but it is *potentially* one; a block of marble is potentially a statue. Change is the process by which what is potential becomes actual.

Actuality is prior to potentiality in every important sense — in definition, in time (an actual oak must precede the acorn that grows from it), and in being. The potential is defined by reference to the actuality it is a potential *for*, and it is the actual that draws the potential into realization. This lets Aristotle define motion itself as "the actuality of the potential *as* potential" — the ongoing realization of a capacity.

The distinction runs through his entire system. It grounds his physics (natural change), his biology (growth as the actualization of a form), and his psychology (the soul as the "first actuality" of a living body). It culminates in his theology: **God** is *pure actuality*, with no unrealized potential whatever, the **unmoved mover** that causes all motion by being the object of desire, drawing every being toward its own fullest realization. Few conceptual pairs have proved so fertile in the history of philosophy.
ideas.Virtue ethics and the golden mean:
In the *Nicomachean Ethics*, Aristotle asks what the good life for a human being is, and answers: **eudaimonia** — flourishing, or living and doing well. This is not a feeling but an activity: the exercise, over a complete life, of the soul's capacities in accordance with **virtue** (*aretē*, excellence). Since reason is the distinctively human function, the good life is a life of activity guided by reason.

Virtues of character are stable dispositions to feel and act well, and they are acquired not by instruction but by **habituation** — we become just by doing just acts, brave by doing brave ones, until right action becomes second nature. Each such virtue, Aristotle famously argues, is a **mean** between two vices, one of excess and one of deficiency. Courage is the mean between recklessness and cowardice; generosity between wastefulness and stinginess; proper pride between vanity and meekness. The mean is not a bland average but the *right* response — the appropriate amount of fear, anger, or spending, "at the right time, toward the right people, for the right reason, in the right way."

Finding the mean requires **practical wisdom** (*phronēsis*), the intellectual virtue of perceiving what a particular situation demands; there is no mechanical rule, and the mean is relative to the person and circumstance. Aristotle's virtue ethics — centered on character, habit, and flourishing rather than rules or consequences — is one of the three great traditions of moral philosophy, and its twentieth-century revival through Alasdair MacIntyre and others is traced in the connection essays.
ideas.Syllogistic logic:
Aristotle founded the discipline of **logic** — the formal study of valid inference — and his achievement was so complete that Kant could still say, two thousand years later, that logic had "not been able to advance a single step" beyond him. His central invention is the **syllogism**: a deductive argument in which a conclusion follows *necessarily* from two premises. The classic example — "All men are mortal; Socrates is a man; therefore Socrates is mortal" — displays the form: from two premises joined by a shared "middle term," a conclusion is drawn with necessity.

Aristotle's genius was to see that validity depends on **form**, not content. He introduced variables (letters standing for terms), classified premises by quantity and quality (universal or particular, affirmative or negative), and worked out systematically which combinations — the "figures" and "moods" — yield valid conclusions and which do not. This was the first formal system in history: the discovery that reasoning itself has a structure that can be studied abstractly, apart from any particular subject matter.

Collected in the works later called the *Organon* ("instrument"), his logic became the backbone of education from antiquity through the Middle Ages, memorized by every schoolboy. It remained essentially unchallenged until the late nineteenth century, when Frege and Russell created modern mathematical logic — which, by handling relations and multiple generality that the syllogism could not express, finally superseded it. Even so, Aristotle's core insight, that logical validity is a matter of form, remains the very foundation of the field.

works.Metaphysics:
The *Metaphysics* is Aristotle's inquiry into the most fundamental questions of reality — and it even gave the field its name, though by accident: the title means "the [books] *after* the *Physics*," their position in the ancient edition. Aristotle himself calls the subject "first philosophy" and defines it as the science of **being *qua* being** — the study not of any particular kind of being, as physics studies natural bodies, but of what it is to *be* anything at all.

The work ranges across the deepest problems: the nature of **substance** (*ousia*), which Aristotle takes to be the primary sense of being and identifies with the individual form-matter compound; the analysis of potentiality and actuality; a sustained critique of Plato's **Theory of Forms**, which he argues cannot explain the very things they are meant to explain; and the principles of identity and non-contradiction, which he defends as the firmest of all axioms.

It culminates, in Book Lambda, in Aristotle's **theology**: the argument for an **unmoved mover**, a being of pure actuality, eternal and immaterial, that causes all motion not by pushing but by being the supreme object of desire — drawing the cosmos toward itself as the beloved draws the lover. This God is "thought thinking itself," engaged eternally in the highest activity, contemplation. Difficult, unfinished, and compiled from separate treatises rather than composed as one book, the *Metaphysics* nonetheless founded ontology and natural theology, and no later metaphysics in the West — scholastic, rationalist, or modern — was written without reference to it.
works.Nicomachean Ethics:
The *Nicomachean Ethics* — likely named for Aristotle's son Nicomachus — is the most important work of moral philosophy from antiquity and a founding text of the entire discipline. It asks a single organizing question: what is the ultimate good for a human being, the end at which a well-lived life aims? Aristotle's answer is **eudaimonia**, usually translated "happiness" but better rendered *flourishing* — not a passing feeling but the activity of living well over a complete life.

From this starting point the work builds a complete ethical theory. It analyzes **virtue** as a stable disposition of character, acquired through habit, lying as a **mean** between extremes of excess and deficiency. It distinguishes virtues of character (courage, justice, generosity, temperance) from virtues of intellect, and gives a subtle account of **practical wisdom** (*phronēsis*), the capacity to discern the right action in particular circumstances. It examines voluntary and involuntary action and moral responsibility, weakness of will (*akrasia* — why we act against our better judgment), the nature of pleasure, and, at length, **friendship**, to which two whole books are devoted.

The work culminates in a famous and much-debated claim: that the highest happiness lies in **contemplation** (*theōria*), the activity of the intellect that is most self-sufficient, most continuous, and most godlike. Practical, humane, and psychologically acute — refusing, as Aristotle says, more precision than the subject allows — the *Nicomachean Ethics* founded virtue ethics and has shaped moral thought through Aquinas's Christian synthesis and its modern revival ever since.
works.Physics:
Aristotle's *Physics* is his foundational study of **nature** (*physis*) — the science of the natural world of things that move and change — and it defined the framework of natural philosophy for nearly two thousand years. Its subject is everything that has within itself a principle of motion and rest: the elements, plants, animals, the heavens. To understand nature, Aristotle argues, is to understand change, and to understand change is to grasp its causes.

The work lays out the machinery of his entire natural philosophy: the analysis of change through **matter and form** and **potentiality and actuality**; the doctrine of the **four causes**; and extended, penetrating discussions of **motion**, **place**, **the void** (which he argues cannot exist), **time** (which he defines as "the number of motion with respect to before and after"), and **infinity**. Throughout, nature is understood **teleologically**: natural things act for ends, and "nature does nothing in vain."

The *Physics* culminates in the argument, completed in the *Metaphysics*, that the eternal motion of the cosmos requires a first **unmoved mover**. Much of its specific physics — that heavier bodies fall faster, that continued motion requires a continuous mover, that there can be no vacuum — was overturned by Galileo and Newton, and the Scientific Revolution largely defined itself against it. Yet its questions remain the questions of the philosophy of physics — what *are* space, time, motion, and cause? — and its teleological conception of nature still frames debates in biology and the philosophy of science.
works.Politics:
Aristotle's *Politics* is the ancient world's most systematic treatise on the state, and the companion to his *Ethics*: where the *Ethics* asks how an individual achieves the good life, the *Politics* asks how a community can be organized so that its citizens can achieve it. Its foundational claim is that the **city-state** (*polis*) is natural, growing from the household and the village as the complete community in which alone human beings can fully flourish — and that "man is by nature a **political animal**" (*zōon politikon*), a being who realizes his nature only in political community.

The work combines empirical and normative inquiry. Aristotle and his school collected and analyzed 158 actual constitutions, and the *Politics* draws on this to classify regimes by who rules and in whose interest: the three "correct" forms (monarchy, aristocracy, polity) that govern for the common good, and their three corrupt counterparts (tyranny, oligarchy, democracy) that serve only the rulers. He argues for the stabilizing value of a large **middle class** and a "mixed" constitution, criticizes Plato's *Republic* (especially its abolition of private property and family), and discusses citizenship, revolution, and education — and, notoriously, defends natural slavery, a stain that modern readers rightly reject.

Grounded in the conviction that politics is a branch of ethics, aimed at the good life rather than mere power or survival, the *Politics* shaped republican and constitutional thought for centuries — the Aristotelian framework of the political animal and the good regime that Machiavelli and Hobbes would later, deliberately, overturn.
